title: Quantum-limited estimation of atmospheric turbulence via spatial mode decomposition
authors: A. Hrebeniuk, M. Klen, I. Karuseichyk, N. Treps, A. A. Semenov
abstract: We establish the ultimate precision limit for estimating the optical spatial coherence radius (Fried parameter) within a quantum metrological framework. In the weak field regime, we show that spatial-mode decomposition -- originally introduced for superresolution imaging -- enables substantially more precise estimation than conventional direct imaging when the receiver aperture is smaller than the coherence radius.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates how precisely one can measure the spatial coherence radius of light passing through turbulent air. It compares two detection methods—Direct Imaging and Spatial Mode Decomposition—using quantum metrology tools like Fisher Information. The key finding is that SpaDe provides a substantially more precise estimation than DI, especially when the turbulence effects are weak.
main_problem: The primary goal is establishing the ultimate quantum precision limit for estimating the optical spatial coherence radius (Fried parameter, $r_0$) of atmospheric turbulence.
main_result: Spatial Mode Decomposition (SpaDe) significantly outperforms conventional Direct Imaging (DI) in the weak-scintillation regime, approaching the theoretical quantum limit when properly implemented.
method: The analysis uses quantum metrology principles, comparing the Fisher Information (FI) derived from DI versus SpaDe measurements against the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI).
model_or_system: The system involves light propagating through a turbulent atmosphere, modeled using single-photon density operators and characterized by field correlation functions ($\Gamma$). The analysis compares two measurement schemes: Direct Imaging (DI) and Spatial Mode Decomposition (SpaDe).
key_observables: Fisher Information (FI), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), spatial coherence radius ($r_0$), and the ratio FI/QFI.
important_parameters: Weak field regime ($\eta n_{th} \ll 1$); normalized parameters $	heta_2$ and $\sigma_2$; the aperture size $D$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the weak-field approximation and often employs a Gaussian approximation for the complex correlation functions to derive analytical results.
figures_summary: Figures compare the ratio of FI to QFI for DI vs. SpaDe, showing SpaDe's superior performance in the weak-scintillation regime; other figures plot these metrics as functions of $r_0/D$.
paper_structure: The paper develops by first establishing the quantum estimation framework using CR bounds and QFI. It then analyzes two measurement techniques (DI vs. SpaDe) under Gaussian approximations, deriving explicit FI expressions for both. Finally, it compares these results against realistic models to demonstrate the advantage of SpaDe.
why_interesting: This work bridges advanced topics in quantum estimation theory with applied physics challenges like atmospheric sensing, providing concrete metrics (FI/QFI) to quantify measurement improvement beyond classical limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 3. Results of the numerical simulation of light prop- agation and subsequent estimation of r0 using binary detec- tion. The variance asymptotically approaches the CR bound for the SpaDe. Parameters: propagation length 1 km, re- fractive index structure constant C2 n = 3.9 × 10−16 m−2/3, wavelength 578 nm, wind velocity 10 m/s, time step 0.2 ms, acquisition time 100 s, mean photon number within the aper- ture νSap = 0.02, and sample size M = 8940. Inset: P(0|r0) vs r0/D, cf. Eq. (18), with sampled points.
FIG. 4. Parameters σ2 and θ2, normalized to (λf/D)2, vs the normalized coherence radius r0/D. The parameters are obtained by maximizing the quantum fidelity and subse- quently fitted by Eq. (12).
